html:
Lumos Diagnostics (7YU.F / ASX: LDX): The Raise Is Obscuring the Repricing Event

Lumos looks like a messy penny-stock diagnostics recapitalization. That is the opportunity. The company just moved FebriDx from a constrained moderate-complexity lab product into the enormous CLIA-waived U.S. point-of-care channel, and the market is still mostly debating dilution.

Current 7YU.F last price is about €0.105 on Frankfurt, while the primary ASX line is around A$0.150, with roughly 923.9 million quoted shares after the May SPP issuance. The Frankfurt line is thin, so the ASX listing is the better economic reference.

The asset is FebriDx, a 10-minute fingerstick lateral-flow test that detects MxA and CRP host-response biomarkers to help distinguish bacterial from non-bacterial acute respiratory infection. This matters because pathogen panels tell you which virus is present, if any. FebriDx addresses the doctor’s real prescribing problem: does this patient need antibiotics?

The FDA event was not incremental. FDA’s CLIA waiver decision summary for FebriDx, effective March 24, 2026, shows a dual 510(k) and CLIA waiver for the FebriDx Bacterial/Non-Bacterial Assay. In the waived-use study, untrained users produced 99.1% positive agreement and 98.4% negative agreement versus trained operators, with all initial invalid tests correctly identified and valid after retesting. That is exactly the data needed to make the product usable in primary care, urgent care, retail clinics, and pharmacies.

Lumos says the waiver expands the U.S. opportunity from about 18,000 sites and 6 million patients to roughly 270,000 to 300,000 sites and 80 million patients, or more than US$1 billion annually. More important than the TAM slide, the commercial machine is finally turning on. Q3 FY26 revenue was US$4.8 million, up 37% year over year, with product revenue of US$2.4 million, up 243%, mostly driven by FebriDx. After the waiver, PHASE Scientific placed Lumos’ largest FebriDx order to date, US$1.3 million, and then paid a US$5.0 million product prepayment under the U.S. distribution agreement.

That PHASE deal is the fulcrum. It is a six-year exclusive U.S. distribution agreement valued at up to US$317 million, including US$308.5 million of minimum order quantities in years 2 through 6 if milestones are hit. The market is right not to value that at face value today, but it is wrong to value it at almost zero after FDA clearance, CLIA waiver, product orders, prepayments, Medicare recognition, and real urgent-care rollout evidence.

The most important early customer is WellStreet. Lumos announced expansion from one pilot site to 43 additional WellStreet urgent care locations, with potential rollout across all 163 locations. WellStreet sees about 1.1 million acute respiratory presentations annually. If FebriDx becomes embedded as a standing-order triage test there, it gives Lumos the operating data, reimbursement data, and provider proof points needed to sell into other urgent-care chains.

Reimbursement is better than the market appreciates. Lumos has Medicare reimbursement recognition from all seven MACs at US$41.38 per test, and management says 5 of 8 national private insurers were paying in the WellStreet setting. The remaining work is private-payer contracting and denial management, but the framework is no longer hypothetical. At US$41 reimbursement, the economics can work for the physician, the distributor, and Lumos. Management’s presentation targets roughly 60% manufacturer gross margin today with expansion toward 80% through volume, supplier pricing, and automation.

The balance sheet is no longer the same story it was in March. Q3 ended with only US$1.1 million of cash and US$2.8 million of operating cash burn, which explains why retail commentary on Reddit and HotCopper has focused on dilution. But post-quarter, Lumos received the US$5.0 million PHASE prepayment, completed an A$20 million placement, raised A$265,000 through the SPP, and announced the US$0.5 million BARDA CLIA-waiver milestone payment. The company is still not overcapitalized, but it has enough oxygen to attempt the U.S. launch rather than returning immediately to survival mode.

There is also non-Febridx ballast. Hologic’s fFN development program, Aptatek’s PheCheck work, and Micro-Pak’s new three-year manufacturing agreement are not the core upside, but they reduce the “single product science project” risk. The Hologic work alone has meaningful remaining milestone value, and Micro-Pak’s first purchase order gives another example of Lumos converting assay-development work into manufacturing revenue.

The variant perception is simple: the market sees a recently diluted ASX microcap whose stock already ran hard from the 2025 lows. The better lens is that Lumos has crossed the hardest regulatory bridge for a unique point-of-care host-response test, has reimbursement scaffolding, has a distributor with prepaid economics, and has early chain-level urgent-care deployment. The stock is not cheap on trailing revenue. It is cheap if FebriDx is now entering its first real commercial cycle.

Base case: FebriDx reaches US$50 million to US$70 million annual revenue over the next three years, with services and partner programs adding another US$8 million to US$12 million. At 4x forward revenue, allowing for further dilution to roughly 1.05 billion shares, the stock is worth about €0.24. That is roughly +129% from €0.105.

Bull case: WellStreet validates broad urgent-care use, PHASE delivers a meaningful portion of its MOQ schedule, pediatric expansion adds addressable use, and a strategic diagnostics buyer starts valuing FebriDx as the scarce CLIA-waived host-response platform. At US$140 million to US$170 million revenue and 5x sales, diluted for growth capital, 7YU.F can trade around €0.75. That is roughly +614%.

Bear case: reimbursement friction remains high, urgent-care adoption stalls after pilots, PHASE orders fail to scale beyond prepaid commitments, and Lumos needs another discounted raise before FebriDx reaches self-funding revenue. In that case, the stock is worth about €0.03, or roughly -71%.

The risk is real, but the asymmetry is better than the chart suggests. At today’s price, investors are paying for a messy microcap launch and getting a newly CLIA-waived, reimbursable, first-mover diagnostic asset with actual commercial pull-through starting to show.
